frameworks.name: Representation Space Traversal Architecture
frameworks.description: A two-system AI architecture where the primary mechanism is explicit traversal of data representation space, rather than treating representation learning as a byproduct of optimisation.

frameworks.name: Data Transformation System
frameworks.description: Travels representation space using reversible graph transformations (translations). Owns and restructures the shared library. Governs layer fusion and splitting. Writes to empirical library only when subgraph occurrence is consistent under converged rules for a given label. Cannot innovate — all transformations reversible. Requires GA-improved rules to produce label-conditioned reproducibility; without rule improvement, consistent subgraph production across images is not achievable.
frameworks.fitness_signal: Label-conditioned reproducibility: given a fixed label, minimize variance in subgraph structures produced across different images with that label. Lower variance = higher fitness. Library entry is a byproduct of GA convergence — subgraphs that consistently appear under converged rules for a given label earn entry.

frameworks.name: Logic Rewriting System
frameworks.description: Improves the travel logic of the data system. Operates irreversibly on rules (innovation permitted). Evolves itself by the same mechanism using match quality as fitness signal. No write access to either library layer or data. Represented as a graph and operated on by the second system. In the base prototype, GA primitives (mutation and crossover on rule graphs) are fixed — self-evolution mechanism is deferred to second system design.
components: GA over rule graphs (mutation + crossover on rule structure)

frameworks.name: Hierarchical Graph Layer Structure
frameworks.description: Multi-layer graph where each layer holds graph structure. Movement between representations is either sideways (within a layer, reversible) or upward (between layers, irreversible). Layer 0 is mutable only during encoding — once a structure is committed, layer 0 is immutable. All further transformation is sideways or upward. The full structure is a traversal graph: nodes are representations at various layers, horizontal edges are reversible rule applications, vertical edges are irreversible transformations with provenance pointers. Finding representations that allow meaningful upward movement is one of the main tasks of the traveler — sideways movement is not bookkeeping, it is the primary computational work.
components: Fusion and splitting (governed by data system)

movement_types.sideways: Reversible rules produce parallel representations within the same layer. No information loss. Multiple equivalent forms coexist, connected by reversible transformations. A sideways transformation is valuable because it opens or closes upward paths — fitness of sideways rules is whether the representations they produce are ones upward rules can fire on. Example: commutativity reordering looks reversible as an operation class but requires provenance to recover original ordering for a specific application.
movement_types.upward: Irreversible rules produce a new graph at layer N+1 derived from a region of layer N. The result is a full graph transformation — not compression to a single node. A provenance pointer connects the layer N+1 graph back to the source region at layer N. The source region is a subgraph (many nodes), not a single node. The source is never destroyed.
movement_types.shortcuts: Discovered paths composing sideways and upward moves that reach the same result via a different route. Finding shortcuts is a primary GA search target.

frameworks.provenance_model: Upward edges carry a pointer to the source graph at layer N. Many-to-one: a subgraph region at layer N collectively produces the layer N+1 result. Maps naturally to hyperedge encoding — a hyperedge over the source region nodes.
frameworks.reversibility_note: Almost everything interesting is irreversible. Commutativity, simplification, solving, normalisation — all destroy ordering or structure information without provenance. The reversible category may not be practically useful and everything may require provenance. Open question pending rule classification work (see q_11, q_12).
frameworks.layer_boundary_definition: A subgraph from layer N becomes a node at layer N+1 when the GA has converged on rules that consistently produce that subgraph for a given label. The boundary is earned through convergence, not assigned by design.
frameworks.implementation_gap: Current arithmetic.py rules are shaped as consuming rewrites — they mutate layer 0 in place and discard the source. Correct shape for encoding phase, wrong shape for transformation phase. operations.py needs provenance tracking: snapshot before irreversible rule application, store snapshot as layer N with link to result graph.

frameworks.name: Rule Space Layer Structure
frameworks.description: Rule space has its own layer structure, independent of the graph hierarchy. The GA can operate at different granularities of rule space. Coarse rules (wildcards) are rule groupings — they delegate to specific micro-rules based on what is actually in the matched subgraph. This is not a substrate feature. The correspondence between graph layer hierarchy and rule layer hierarchy should align: a coarse graph match at layer N maps to a rule group, and the micro-rules in that group are exactly the rules valid for the layer 0 substructures the layer N node points to.
frameworks.wildcard_semantics: A wildcard match fires at the rule-group level. Selection of which specific rule applies is a local decision driven by subgraph content. Reduces visible rule count at the coarse level without eliminating the specific cases — they live inside the group.
frameworks.ga_search_decomposition: Upper-layer rule structure is one GA search target. Micro-rule sets are another. The mapping between them is a third. These can evolve independently or co-evolve with different mutation rates.

frameworks.name: Quantum Traveler Exploration Model
frameworks.description: The traveler explores representation space in parallel — a population of growing structures rather than a single path-finder. Each layer N representation is a potential root for upward growth. The structure branches like moss or a tree: some branches reach a good abstraction and keep growing, some terminate, some find the solution label early and exit. The overall shape is organic, not directed. The system is not searching for the path — it is growing until something matches.

exploration_properties.early_exit: A branch that reaches the solution label cleanly terminates. First-class success outcome, not just path completion. Strong fitness signal for the rules that enabled it.
exploration_properties.dead_ends: Branches that stop growing are normal and cheap. No backtracking needed. Unexploited roots remain available.
exploration_properties.pruning: Structural explosion — runaway node/edge growth — signals a branch moving away from useful abstraction. Pruned before closest-match ranking. Pruning heuristic does not require a precise distance metric, only detection of obvious divergence.
exploration_properties.closest_match_fallback: If no branch reaches the solution label, rank survivors by proximity. The metric only needs to rank survivors, not measure absolute distance. Much easier problem than a full distance metric.

frameworks.ga_fitness_connection: Rules that produce more branches reaching the solution label, or reaching it faster, are fitter. Rules that consistently produce dead-end patches are less fit. Evaluation is on growth patterns, not individual transformations.

frameworks.name: Compound Match Resolution
frameworks.description: When multiple rules fire in a single pass and their match regions overlap, the overlapping regions are resolved as a compound match rather than independent firings. This prevents unbounded layer splitting while preserving the information from all firing rules.
frameworks.firing_model: Single firing pass per ruleset application. Each irreversible match produces a new graph as a delta — unchanged nodes are references to the origin layer (structural sharing, not full copy). Reversible rules that overlap are reclassified as irreversible for that firing — reversibility is context-dependent, not just a property of the rule in isolation.

resolution_algorithm.step_1: Find all overlapping match regions across the full firing set.
resolution_algorithm.step_2: Rank by overlap degree — number of rules sharing nodes in that region.
resolution_algorithm.step_3: Resolve highest degree overlap first — produce compound output nodes for that region.
resolution_algorithm.step_4: Resolved output nodes become stable anchors, equivalent to outside nodes, for remaining unresolved matches.
resolution_algorithm.step_5: Cascade down degree ranking until all overlaps resolved.
resolution_algorithm.step_6: Route boundary connections through resolved regions to outside nodes.

node_types_in_resolution.outside_nodes: Never transformed by definition. Only their connections change. Stable anchors throughout resolution.
node_types_in_resolution.overlapping_nodes: Produce two output nodes, one per rule. Clean — already expressible in current formalism.
node_types_in_resolution.common_output_nodes: Receive connections from both transformations. No conflict — union of connections.
node_types_in_resolution.boundary_of_one_inside_other: The crux case. A node that is a boundary (outside connection) from rule A's perspective but has been transformed by rule B. Resolution: rule B's output node that replaced the shared boundary node inherits rule A's external connection information. Compound transition graph threads rule A's boundary connections through rule B's output.

frameworks.bridging_node_case: A node bridging two equal-degree overlap regions surfaces naturally as a higher-degree overlap and is caught in step 2 before either region. Algorithm handles this without explicit tie-breaking.
frameworks.formalism: Compound transition graph is a composition operation on two transition graphs sharing a boundary. Expressible as an extension of the current triple (Graph 1, Graph 2S, Graph 2O) formalism — not a new substrate requirement.

frameworks.name: Layer Generation Granularity
frameworks.description: Layers are generated per compound firing pass, not per individual rule application. A stable configuration or solution match is the natural layer boundary. Unchanged nodes are structural references to the origin layer — layers are deltas, not clones.
frameworks.bitwise_arithmetic_note: Bitwise arithmetic is a pathological case for layer generation — high firing density, maximum overlap, low semantic lift per firing. Produces many layers relative to semantic content. Acceptable from the machine's perspective but not a representative benchmark for layer dynamics.
frameworks.maturation_trajectory: An inexperienced traveler descends to bit-level resolution for every operation. A mature traveler with library access recognizes multi-bit patterns early and operates at a coarser granularity, only descending to single-bit resolution when forced by the final result. This is not programmed behavior — it emerges from fitness pressure. Travel paths that avoid unnecessary bit-level descent produce cleaner layer structures and are measurably fitter.
frameworks.fitness_gradient: The difference in layer efficiency between naive bit-level thrashing and library-assisted multi-bit operation is large and measurable. Bitwise arithmetic domain is therefore a good training ground precisely because this gradient is clear.

frameworks.name: System Endpoint Architecture
frameworks.description: The mature form of the Perspective system. Three self-similar levels plus a human gate. Each level runs the same fundamental mechanism — graph-based exploration with a population of variants — at a different abstraction scale.
frameworks.input_stage: Data arrives as a graph. Perspective looks for structural matches against known library representations — either prompted by a hint or searching autonomously if library access is permitted. The memory plus input problem is encoded as a graph paired with a travel path, refined over many iterations. The starting point is a positioned structure with prior knowledge of how to begin moving.

levels.traveler: Explores representation space. Has matured past blind GA variation — reads its input and predicts productive travel attempts rather than sampling randomly. Uses library lookup for known solutions and known travel paths. Operates under overseer population management.
levels.overseer: Manages a population of traveler variants. Creates variants based on observed growth patterns. Directs population toward faster and cleaner goal reach. Reads traveler travel paths as data using the same graph matching machinery.
levels.senior_overseer: Manages overseer variants. Searches for its own inheritor. Retires only after exhaustive degradation testing has confirmed the inheritor does not regress on any covered test. Succession safety is the senior's primary responsibility — the burden of proof is on the inheritor, not on the senior finding a reason to stay.
levels.human: Holds the threshold gate. Decides when the reasoned library unlocks. Approves senior succession. Deliberately outside the system — the fourth level is not automated.

frameworks.travel_paths_as_data: Travel paths are first-class data structures encoded as transition graphs in the same formalism as everything else. Library entries are representations paired with the travel paths that reliably reach them. The overseer reads traveler travel paths as data. The senior reads overseer travel paths as data. The self-similarity is literal — the same matching and rewriting machinery runs at every level because the input is always graphs and transition graphs.
frameworks.self_similarity_note: The formalism choice at the base — two edge types, directed graphs, transition graph triples — had to be expressive enough to represent its own operation history without a separate metalanguage. That constraint is load-bearing for the endpoint architecture.

frameworks.name: Hybrid Representation Model
frameworks.description: Previously considered — rejected. Leaf attributes are counter to design philosophy. Complexity is absorbed upward through the layer hierarchy into compressed subgraph nodes, not stored as primitive attributes. Continuous-to-discrete boundary handled at input layer via quantization.

frameworks.name: Human Capability Threshold Gate
frameworks.description: Human evaluates system readiness before unlocking the Reasoned Library. Threshold is NOT delegated to the system. Candidate criteria: accuracy across minimum distinct tasks, generalisation outside training distribution, internal consistency of logic-derived entries validated against labels.

frameworks.name: Base Substrate Architecture
frameworks.description: The graph formalism used by both the Data Transformation System and the second meta-level system. Modular and scalable — base prototype uses directed graphs with binary edge typing. Richer substrates are named upgrade paths evaluated empirically against measured limitations of the base.

decisions.directionality: Directed — all edges are directed. Encodes causality, transformation order, and hierarchy direction.
decisions.edge_types: Exactly two types, globally fixed, no extensions permitted at this level. Type 1: structural — defines what a pattern is (composition, identity). Type 2: operational — defines transformations between patterns, and serves as cursor/pointer in rule firing. Reduces isomorphism complexity by allowing regime-separated matching. Binary typing does not clutter rule space.
decisions.node_typing: None. Typing complicates rule space. Identity emerges from structural relationships and library compression, not labels.
decisions.weights: Not in base prototype. Named upgrade path — to be evaluated empirically.
decisions.hyperedges: Not in base prototype. Named upgrade path — motivated by CA-derived insight that discrete systems have fragmented representation spaces with unreachable configurations, and by travel path encoding argument (see below). To be evaluated empirically via Phase 6 of prototype roadmap.
decisions.automation: Substrate upgrade decisions are made manually by the researcher at the start of a training run, not automated at runtime.

frameworks.two_level_principle: Every substrate decision must be evaluated twice — once for the traveler operating on data, once for the second system operating on the traveler. Features that only serve one level do not belong in the shared base.
frameworks.hyperedge_structural_argument: Transitions operate over regions of layer N (multiple nodes), not single nodes. A binary edge from a representative node to the result is a lossy encoding of what actually happened. If travel paths cannot be faithfully encoded at k=2, the self-similarity property breaks — the traveler cannot operate on its own history with the same machinery. This makes hyperedges a structural requirement for the endpoint architecture, not just an expressiveness improvement. The only open question is when to implement, not whether. Blocked by isomorphism cost — see q_07, q_08.

frameworks.name: Graph Rewrite Rule Representation
frameworks.description: Rules are represented as triples of graphs. Graph 1 (match graph): mixed edge types, used for pattern matching against target. Graph 2S (structural transition graph): single structural edge type, specifies structural changes after matched structural edges are stripped. Graph 2O (operational transition graph): single operational edge type, specifies new operational edges to add after transition. Output is always clean — no mixed edges in output. The triple schema is required because Graph 2 must be able to express both structural and operational output edges, and a single-type graph cannot encode both without ambiguity or violating the binary edge constraint.
rule_firing_sequence: 1. Match Graph 1 (mixed) against target graph — produces node mapping from pattern nodes to target nodes
2. Strip one edge type from the matched subgraph (produces clean single-type subgraph)
3. Apply Graph 2S (structural transition) to the cleaned subgraph
4. Apply Graph 2O (operational transition) to add new operational edges
5. Reattach stripped edges to output nodes via node mapping
frameworks.node_mapping: Explicit mapping from input nodes to output nodes in the rule. Required for reattachment of stripped edges and for applying Graph 2O correctly. Operational edges serve as node pointers in the rule — when input is an operational graph, structural edges take the pointer role instead (role swap handled at match time by interpreter).
frameworks.edge_role_symmetry: Structural and operational edge roles are symmetric and positional, not fundamental. Role swap is an interpreter convention, not a change to rule structure. GA operates on each graph independently — no pairing complexity at GA level.
frameworks.ga_compatibility: Triple schema keeps each graph uniform for GA operations. Mutation and crossover operate on Graph 1, Graph 2S, and Graph 2O independently.
frameworks.bias_testing_note: For early bias testing only: Graph 2O can be omitted and new operational edges hardcoded as a post-processing step in the interpreter. This is valid only while testing matching and structural rewrite machinery in isolation. Must be removed before P5 — hardcoded operational edge logic is imperative code smuggled through the back door and becomes permanent scaffolding if left in.

frameworks.name: Graph Matching Design
frameworks.description: Pattern matching is subgraph isomorphism over directed graphs with binary edge types. The match graph (Graph 1) contains both structural and operational edges. Matching finds a mapping from pattern nodes to target graph nodes such that all pattern edges exist in the target under the mapping, preserving edge type and direction.
frameworks.algorithm: Subgraph isomorphism via backtracking search with constraint propagation. Complexity is O(n!) in worst case — acceptable for small graphs in prototype, flagged for profiling at P6.
frameworks.edge_type_handling: Matching is edge-type-aware. A structural edge in the pattern must map to a structural edge in the target; an operational edge in the pattern must map to an operational edge in the target. This halves the effective search space by separating structural and operational constraint satisfaction.
frameworks.role_swap: At match time, the interpreter determines which edge type plays which role based on the input graph composition. If the input graph is predominantly operational (e.g. a rule graph being operated on by the second system), structural edges take the pointer role and operational edges take the composition role. The swap is an interpreter convention applied before matching begins — the matching algorithm itself is role-agnostic.
frameworks.match_result: A match result is a bijective node mapping: pattern_node -> target_node. Multiple valid mappings may exist (match_all). The mapping is the basis for rule application — Graph 2S and Graph 2O are applied using this mapping to locate the correct target nodes.
frameworks.match_all: match_all() returns all valid mappings. Required for rules that fire repeatedly at different positions in the same graph (e.g. tombstone GC, carry propagation). The traversal loop in P5 calls match_all and applies rules iteratively until no matches remain.
frameworks.negative_constraints: Current match() has no support for negative structural constraints (matching nodes with no incoming edges, or absence of a specific edge). This is a known limitation. Workaround: use tombstone markers to make absence conditions positive and matchable.
frameworks.complexity_flag: O(n!) worst case. For the prototype scope (small arithmetic expressions), this is acceptable. Flagged for P6 profiling — if graph size grows enough that match() dominates runtime, pruning heuristics or VF2 algorithm should be evaluated.

frameworks.name: Graph Operation Interface
frameworks.description: Abstraction layer over graph operations. The traveler and second system interact with graphs only through this interface — no substrate assumptions above it. Enables substrate swapping without touching core logic.

transformation.note: Reversibility guaranteed at interface level regardless of substrate.

substrate_introspection.description: Capability reporting only — upgrade decisions are manual.

frameworks.name: Second System (Meta-level)
frameworks.description: Represents the traveler as a graph and evolves it using graph rewriting — the same substrate and interface as the object level. Currently open in design. Uses the same graph operation interface as the Data Transformation System.
frameworks.notes: Design deliberately deferred. The traveler's actual behavior should inform the second system's design rather than prescribing it upfront. Self-similarity across levels is a goal — same formalism at both object and meta level.
